summary: 17th-century French Baroque painter known for candlelit chiaroscuro scenes. Known for The Penitent Magdalene (woman with skull reflecting candle in mirror), The Cheat with the Ace of Diamonds/Clubs, The Fortune Teller, Joseph the Carpenter. Influenced by Caravaggio and the Utrecht Caravaggisti (especially Gerrit van Honthorst). Based in Luneville, patronized by both Louis XIII and Louis XIV. Works forgotten until 1915 rediscovery by Herman Voss. Also painted many hurdy-gurdy players.

works.name: The Penitent Magdalene
works.description: Woman with skull on lap staring into mirror reflecting candlelight. Also Magdalene with the Smoking Flame (similar scene with smoking flame). The Magdalene appears extremely emaciated. Appeared in The Little Mermaid (Ariel's grotto). Wikipedia

works.name: The Cheat (two versions)
works.description: Cheat with Ace of Diamonds and Cheat with Ace of Clubs. Three people collude against a foppish youth; card sharp retrieves card from belt. Shifty woman in feathered hat. The Ace of Clubs version is at the Kimbell Art Museum. Wikipedia

works.name: St. Sebastian Tended by St. Irene
works.description: St. Irene holds a large torch while tending to St. Sebastian. Typical candlelit scene.

works.name: Hurdy-Gurdy Paintings
works.description: Multiple paintings of hurdy-gurdy players, including blind players. Some attributed to the mysterious 'Hurdy-Gurdy Master.' The Hurdy-Gurdy Player features a man with a vulgarly open mouth playing the instrument.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Based in Luneville, patronized by Louis XIII and Louis XIV. Works largely forgotten until 1915 article by Herman Voss. Influenced by Caravaggio and the Utrecht Caravaggisti (Gerrit van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen). Other works: The Fortune Teller (boy's purse and watch stolen), Joseph the Carpenter, The Musician's Brawl/Quarrel (hurdy-gurdy players fighting), The Newborn Christ, The Flea Catcher. Fortune Teller at center of forgery controversy at Met.

clues.clue: Candle-lit interior scenes; chiaroscuro influenced by Caravaggio and the Utrecht Caravaggisti (especially Gerrit van Honthorst)

comprehensive_summary:
Georges de la Tour (1593-1652) was a 17th-century French Baroque painter based in Luneville, patronized by both Louis XIII and Louis XIV. He is best known for his candle-lit interior chiaroscuro scenes, heavily influenced by Caravaggio and the Utrecht Caravaggisti, especially Gerrit van Honthorst and Hendrick ter Brugghen. His works were largely forgotten until a 1915 article by German art historian Herman Voss.

His most iconic painting, The Penitent Magdalene, shows a woman with a skull on her lap staring into a mirror reflecting candlelight. A variant, Magdalene with the Smoking Flame, appeared in The Little Mermaid (in Ariel's grotto). The 1640 version depicts the Magdalene as extremely emaciated.

The Cheat exists in two versions (Ace of Diamonds and Ace of Clubs), showing three people colluding against a foppish youth as a card sharp retrieves a card from his belt, with a shifty woman in a feathered (sometimes red-feathered) cap. The Ace of Clubs version is at the Kimbell Art Museum.

De la Tour painted multiple hurdy-gurdy players, including blind players. Some of these have been attributed to the mysterious 'Hurdy-Gurdy Master.' The Hurdy-Gurdy Player features a man with a vulgarly open mouth. In The Musician's Brawl (or Quarrel), hurdy-gurdy players fight, one squeezing lemon juice into a blind beggar's eye, and the leftmost fighter defends himself with a hurdy-gurdy.

St. Sebastian Tended by St. Irene shows St. Irene holding a large torch. The Fortune Teller shows a boy having his purse and watch stolen while his fortune is read, and was at the center of a forgery controversy at the Met. Other works include Joseph the Carpenter (also painted as The Dream of Saint Joseph, which Anton Raphael Mengs also painted), The Newborn Christ, and The Flea Catcher.
